ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Yong Tau Foo; mapped to the nearest parent class 'mixed dish (food)' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dishes — soups and stews with starchy staple
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Yong Tau Foo. A0FJP (composite mixed dish with starchy staple) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a soup-based mixed dish served with rice or noodles.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Yong Tau Foo as of 2026.

main_ingredients: white rice or thick rice noodles (beehoon)
allergens: gluten (possible in fish paste or noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Yong Tau Foo with clear soup and rice or thick beehoon is approximately 400 g, comprising a selection of 6–8 stuffed or filled items in clear broth alongside a portion of white rice or thick rice noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo, clear soup, with rice/noodles — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup, with rice/noodles), CR ID F08010064. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; estimated at 0.5 g/100g based on the fat composition of typical fish paste and tofu components using USDA FoodData Central analogues.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles and mixed soup dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup with rice/noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated as a weighted composite: thick rice noodles (beehoon) GI ~53 (University of Sydney GI Database, rice vermicelli entry) and white rice GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database), blended with the low-GI protein and vegetable components of the dish. The composite dish GI is estimated at 45 (low), consistent with the diluting effect of protein, fibre, and broth on glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 33.4) / 100 ≈ 15.0, rounded to 15.2 accounting for serving size of 400 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 293 mg/100g (approximately 1,172 mg per 400 g serving), approaching 50% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal, with limited potassium and fibre to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, avoid soy-based dipping sauces, and limit portion size to keep sodium intake within daily targets; choosing whole-grain noodles over white rice or plain beehoon will also improve the fibre-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 8.34 g/100g (approximately 33 g per 400 g serving) from refined white rice or thick beehoon, with dietary fibre only 0.56 g/100g, resulting in a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 15 per serving despite the composite GI of 45.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or whole-grain noodles to lower the glycaemic response, reduce the rice or noodle portion in favour of additional vegetable and tofu items, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the moderate glycaemic load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is low at 1.96 g/100g with estimated saturated fat of 0.5 g/100g; however, sodium at 293 mg/100g is a relevant cardiovascular risk factor at typical serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yong Tau Foo in clear soup is a relatively heart-friendly option due to its low fat content, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium broth, avoid dipping sauces, and choose whole-grain carbohydrate bases to further support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potential nightshade vegetables (eggplant, chili) in the selection may trigger solanine-related inflammation in RA-susceptible individuals; refined carbohydrate base contributes to glycaemic load-driven inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid nightshade items (eggplant, chili) in their selection and choose whole-grain noodles to reduce refined carbohydrate-driven inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo (clear soup, with rice or noodles) is a popular Singaporean Chinese customisable mixed dish consisting of a self-selected assortment of tofu, fish-paste-stuffed vegetables, fish balls, and other items simmered in a light, clear broth, served alongside white rice or thick rice noodles (beehoon). The dish originates from Hakka Chinese culinary tradition and is widely consumed across Singapore as a hawker-centre staple for lunch and dinner. The clear broth version is notably lighter than the curry or tomato-based variants, with a mild, savoury flavour profile and soft, yielding textures from the tofu and stuffed vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish provides 65 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (3.33 g/100g) from fish paste and tofu, low fat (1.96 g/100g), and moderate carbohydrate (8.34 g/100g) primarily from the rice or noodle component. Dietary fibre is low at 0.56 g/100g. Sodium is the primary clinical concern at 293 mg/100g, reaching approximately 1,172 mg per standard 400 g serving. The composite glycaemic index is estimated at 45 (low), though the glycaemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 15 due to the carbohydrate volume from the starchy base. The dish is suitable for weight management and general healthy eating when the rice or noodle portion is controlled and dipping sauces are avoided. It is not suitable for strict low-sodium diets without modification. Commonly abbreviated as YTF in Singapore.

ingredient_synonyms.beehoon: thick rice noodles, rice vermicelli, bee hoon, bihun
ingredient_synonyms.fish paste: minced fish filling, fish surimi stuffing
ingredient_synonyms.bitter gourd: bitter melon, balsam pear, fu gua

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F08010064), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The exact dish entry is present in the HPB database with a confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with clear documentation of the estimation basis.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues for fish paste and tofu components, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Glycaemic load per serving calculated at 400 g serving size: (45 × 33.36 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 15.2. 5. The dish is customisable; actual nutrient values will vary depending on item selection, portion of rice or noodles, and broth volume. 6. Dipping sauces (typically chili sauce or soy sauce) are not included in the per-100g values and will add sodium. 7. Dietary tag 'vegetarian' from the input record is removed as the dish typically contains fish paste and fish balls; it is not vegetarian unless specifically requested without fish-based items.
